questions.question: Explain the basic data types available in C programming with their typical sizes.
keyPoints: int - integer values, typically 4 bytes
float - floating point, typically 4 bytes with 6-7 digit precision
double - double precision floating point, typically 8 bytes with 15-16 digit precision
char - character storage, typically 1 byte
void - represents absence of type

questions.question: What is the difference between signed and unsigned integers? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Signed integers can represent both positive and negative values
Unsigned integers represent only non-negative values (0 and positive)
Signed uses one bit for sign, effectively halving positive range
Example: signed int range is -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647
Example: unsigned int range is 0 to 4,294,967,295

questions.question: What is type casting in C? Differentiate between implicit and explicit type casting.
keyPoints: Type casting converts one data type to another
Implicit casting is automatic by compiler (type promotion)
Explicit casting uses cast operator: (type)expression
Implicit examples: int + float = float, char = int
Explicit examples: (int)3.14 converts to 3, (float)10/4 = 2.5

questions.question: Write a C program to demonstrate the use of sizeof operator with different data types.
keyPoints: Include stdio.h header
Use sizeof(variable) or sizeof(data_type)
Print sizes using %zu format specifier
Show sizes for char, int, float, double
Demonstrate that sizes vary based on system architecture

questions.question: What is an enumerated data type (enum) in C? How is it declared and used?
keyPoints: Enum is a user-defined data type consisting of integer constants
Declared using enum keyword followed by name and curly braces
Default first value is 0, subsequent values increment by 1
Can assign custom values during declaration
Improves code readability by using meaningful names

questions.question: Explain the concept of type conversion in C with examples. Discuss implicit conversion rules and demonstrate explicit type casting.
keyPoints: Type conversion changes one data type to another
Implicit conversion: compiler automatically converts types in expressions
Conversion hierarchy: char/short -> int -> unsigned int -> long -> unsigned long -> float -> double -> long double
Explicit casting: using (type) before expression
Examples of implicit: int + float = float, char + int = int
Examples of explicit: (int)3.14159 gives 3, (float)10/4 gives 2.5
Discuss data loss in narrowing conversions

questions.question: Discuss the different modifiers used with integer data types in C. Explain how short, long, signed, and unsigned modifiers affect the range and memory usage of integer types.
keyPoints: short int: minimum 2 bytes, used for smaller integers
long int: at least 4 bytes, on 64-bit may be 8 bytes
long long int: at least 8 bytes for very large numbers
signed (default): uses two's complement, includes negative values
unsigned: only non-negative values, doubles positive range
short range: -32,768 to 32,767 (signed)
unsigned long range: 0 to 4,294,967,295

questions.question: Write a C program that declares variables of different data types, initializes them, and prints their sizes and values. Also demonstrate type conversion between different data types.
keyPoints: Declare char, int, float, double variables
Use const for constant values
Print using appropriate format specifiers
Demonstrate implicit conversion in expressions
Demonstrate explicit casting with examples
Show how precision is affected in conversions
Use sizeof to display memory sizes

questions.question: Explain the concept of constants in C. Differentiate between const keyword and #define preprocessor directive with examples.
keyPoints: Constants are variables whose value cannot be changed
const keyword: creates compile-time constants with type checking
#define: preprocessor directive for macro substitution
const occupies memory, #define does not
const has scope/block-level, #define is file-wide until #undef
Example: const int MAX = 100; vs #define MAX 100
const allows debugging, #define does not
const respects data types, #define does not
